pages.text: Strength and drying shrinkage properties of self-compacting concretes incorporating multi-system blended mineral admixtures Erhan Güneyisi a, Mehmet Gesog˘ lu a, Erdog˘ an Özbay b,* a Department of Civil Engineering, Gaziantep University, 27310 Gaziantep, Turkey b Department of Civil Engineering, Mustafa Kemal University, Iskenderun, Hatay, Turkey article info Article history: Received 1 December 2009 Received in revised form 12 March 2010 Accepted 1 April 2010 Available online 20 April 2010 Keywords: Fly ash Ground granulated blast furnace slag Silica fume Metakaolin Shrinkage abstract Drying shrinkage can be a major reason for the deterioration of concrete structures. The contraction of the material is normally hindered by either internal or external restraints so that tensile stresses are induced. These stresses may exceed the tensile strength and cause concrete to crack. The present study investi- gated compressive strength and particularly drying shrinkage properties of self-compacting concretes containing binary, ternary, and quaternary blends of Portland cement, ﬂy ash (FA), ground granulated blast furnace slag (GGBFS), silica fume (SF), and metakaolin (MK). For this purpose, a total of 65 self-com- pacting concrete (SCC) mixtures were prepared at two different water to binder ratios. It was observed that drying shrinkage lessened with the use of FA, GGBFS, and MK while incorporation of SF increased the drying shrinkage. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Drying shrinkage can be deﬁned as the volumetric change due to the drying of concrete. This change in volume of the concrete is not equal to volume of the water lost. The loss of free water oc- curs ﬁrst; this causes little to no shrinkage. As the drying of the concrete continues, the adsorbed water held by hydrostatic tension in the small capillaries (<50 nm) is removed. The shrinkage due to this water loss is signiﬁcantly larger than that associated with the loss of free water [1]. The loss of the water produces tensile stres- ses, which lead the concrete to shrink. Previous studies carried out on conventional concrete [2–7] have shown that the shrinkage of concrete introduces characteristic problems in reinforced concrete members and, if not accurately accounted for at the design stage, can cause severe cracking which in turn leads to service life reduc- tion and impair the structural reliability. The addition of pozzolans and slag cement generally increases the pore reﬁnement of the concrete. This creates smaller pores, and drying shrinkage is di- rectly associated with the water held in the smaller pores [8]. The information in the literature about the inﬂuence of mineral admixtures on the drying shrinkage mechanism is found to be somewhat contradictory. Some researchers [9,10] stated that incorporation of mineral admixtures such as low-lime and high- lime ﬂy ashes reduced the drying shrinkage in comparison with the SCC mixtures without ﬂy ash. They also stated that a possible mechanism contributing to the reduction of drying shrinkage in SCCs is the matrix densiﬁcation due to FA addition, which may pre- vent internal moisture evaporation. An alternative mechanism is that unhydrated FA particles serve as ﬁne aggregates to restrain the shrinkage deformation [9,11,12]. Moreover, Jianyong and Yan [13] stated that ultra-ﬁne GGBFS and SF will greatly increase the amount of AFt hydrates and C–S–H gel hydrates as well as the den- sity of hardened cement p

the gaps of reinforcement and corners of moulds without any need for vibration and compaction during the placing process. However, in comparison with conventional concrete, SCC contains larger quantities of mineral ﬁllers, higher quantities of high-range water-reducing admixtures, and the maximum size of the coarse aggregate is smaller. These modiﬁcations in the composition of the mixture affect the behavior of the concrete in its hardened state, including the shrinkage deformations. It is of general accep- tance that shrinkage depends mainly on the characteristics of the paste and the aggregate volume [17]. The volume of paste of a con- crete mixture is usually deﬁned as the sum of the volumes of ce- ment, water, mineral addition and chemical admixtures. Increasing the volume of paste improves ﬂowability but it may have unexpected effects on mechanical properties and time- dependent deformations. For example, drying shrinkage is known to increase with the volume of paste [18,19]. Hwang and Khayat [20] stated that SCCs can develop higher shrinkage compared with conventional concrete as a results of its greater volume of cement paste and relatively low coarse aggregate content. A number of studies have been reported in the literature con- cerning the use of mineral admixtures to enhance the performance of conventional concretes exposed to drying [21]. Jianyong and Yan [13] studied the effect of ultra-ﬁne ground granulated blast furnace and silica fume on the creep and drying shrinkage of high perfor- mance concrete. They concluded that binary use of Portland cement and ultra-ﬁne ground granulated blast furnace slag decreased the drying shrinkage of the concretes with respect to the plain Portland cement concrete. In the work of Teorenau and Nicolescu [22] it was found that the shrinkage of ﬂy ash concrete was much less than that of the plain control concrete, especially the effect being more pro- nounced at higher amounts of ﬂy ash. Ravindrarajah et al. [23] investigated the effect of silica fume content on the drying shrink- age of high strength concrete. They reported that incorporating 8% of silica fume increased the 100 day drying shrinkage of the speci- mens by 17%, whereas the drying shrinkage reduced by about 9% when the silica fume content increased to 15%. Güneyisi et al. [24] conducted an experimental work to investigate the inﬂuence of metakolin on the strength, drying shrinkage, and pore structure of the concrete. They reported that the inclusion of MK remarkably reduced drying shrinkage strain, but increased the strengths of the concretes in varying magnitudes, depending mainly on the replace- ment level of MK, water/binder (w/b) ratio, and age of the testing. It was also reported in the study of Brooks and Megat Johari [25] that using of MK reduced the total shrinkage by about 50%. In the literature, however, there are few works focusing on the drying shrinkage of self-compacting concretes made with the min- eral admixtures. Bouzoubaa and Lachemi [26] studied the effect of high volumes of ﬂy ash on the drying shrinkage of self-compacting concretes. They stated that no difference was noticed between dry- ing shrinkage of Portland control concrete without ﬂy ash and self- compacting concretes with ﬂy ash. Gesog ˘ lu et al. [27] conducted an experiment study to investigate the drying shrinkage of the SCCs produced with the binary, ternary, and quaternary blends of the ﬂy ash, ground granulated slag and silica fume. They clearl

2.2. Concrete mixture proportioning A total of 65 concrete mixtures were designed at two water-bin- der ratios of 0.32 and 0.44 with total cementitious materials con- tent of 550 and 450 kg/m 3, respectively. Table 3 summarizes the mixture proportioning of SCCs. As seen in the Table 3 that the ﬁrst 43 of the mixtures (M1–M43) were produced with 550 kg/m 3 total binder content and 0.32 water-binder ratio while the mixtures from M44 to M65 were produced with the 450 kg/m 3 total binder content and 0.44 water-binder ratio. The plain concrete mixtures included only ordinary Portland cement (PC) as the binder while the remaining mixtures incorporated binary (PC + FA, PC + GGBFS, PC + SF, PC + MK), ternary (PC + FA + GGBFS, PC + FA + SF, PC + FA + MK, PC + GGBFS + SF, PC + GGBFS + MK, PC + SF + MK), and quater- nary (PC + FA + GGBFS + SF, PC + FA + SF + MK, PC + FA + GGBFS + MK, PC + GGBFS + SF + MK) cementitious blends in which a propor- tion of Portland cement was replaced with the mineral admixtures. The replacement ratios for both FA and GGBFS were 20%, 40%, and 60% while those of SF and MK were 5%, 10%, and 15% by weight of the total binder. 2.3. Concrete casting and test specimens In the production of SCC mixtures the mixing sequence and duration are very important. Thus, the procedure for batching and mixing proposed by Khayat et al. [30] was employed to supply the same homogeneity and uniformity in all mixtures. The con- cretes were designed to give a slump ﬂow of 70 ± 3 cm which was achieved by using the superplasticizer at varying amounts. For this, trial batches were produced for each mixture till the de- sired slump ﬂow was obtained. In order to monitor the compres- sive strength, six 150 mm cube samples were produced for each concrete mixture. Specimens were cast full without any compac- tion and vibration. After 24 h casting, they were demoulded and stored in lime saturated water until the age 28 days. Three of the cube specimens were tested at 28 days, the remaining samples were kept in a curing room of 20 ± 2 /C176C and 65 ± 5% relative humid- ity till the testing at 90 days. Compressive strength test was per- formed according to the ASTM C 39 [31] by means of a 3000 kN capacity testing machine. For monitoring the drying shrinkage of the SCCs, four 70 /C270 /C2280 mm prisms were used as per ASTM C 157 [32]. Immediately after demoulding the concrete specimens, the gage length was formed by gluing pins on the surface of the specimens. The initial gage length was measured and consecutive readings were carried out at every 24 h for the ﬁrst 3 weeks and then three times a week. Specimens were maintained in a LEEC humidity cab- inet at 23 /C176C and 50% relative humidity. The length change was measured by means of a dial gage extonsometer with 200 mm gage length which had a capability of measuring 0.002 strain. 3. Experimental results and discussions 3.1. Fresh properties Fresh properties of SCCs were observed through slump ﬂow diameter, T 50 slump ﬂow time, V-funnel ﬂow time, and L-box height ratio according to EFNARC [33]. Slump ﬂow diameters of all mixtures were kept in the range of 670–730 mm throughout the study. The slump ﬂow durations (T 50) of all produced concretes were less than 7 s. The time measured via the V-funnel ﬂow was in the range of 6.3–37 s whilst H 2/H1 ratio ranged from 0.704 to 0.976 depending mainly on the w/b ratio, type and amount of the min- eral admixture used in the concrete production. Test results of t

sured response such that higher the contribution, the effectiveness of the factors to that particular response was higher. Likewise, if the percent contribution is low, the contribution of the factors to that particular response is less [34]. As seen in Table 5 that the p-level of each mineral admixture for drying shrinkage was less than the critical 0.05 value. Therefore, all mineral admixtures used in the SCC production are statistically sig- niﬁcant at a 95% conﬁdence limit. This result suggests that the var- iability of drying shrinkage must be explained in terms of the mineral admixtures used. Hence, the use of SF appeared to be the most effective mineral admixture on the drying shrinkage of SCCs. As clearly seen in Table 5 that the contribution of SF in the varia- tion of the drying shrinkage was 41.4% while that of MK, FA, and GGBFS were 15.7%, 10.7%, and 6.2%, respectively. In the analysis of variance calculations on the compressive strength, 90 days test results were used to obtain the meaningful results. When the re- sults of analysis of variance for compressive strength was evalu- ated, it was observed that ground granulated blast furnace slag was the insigniﬁcant factor on that property. If the test results of compressive strength were examined in more detail, it was ob- served that this property did not show considerable variation with the binary, ternary and quaternary use of GGBFS mineral admix- ture. The most effective factor on the compressive strength of the produced SCCs was the FA with a contribution of 26.7%. The contri- butions of the MK and SF on compressive strength were 12.7% and 9.7%, respectively. 4. Conclusions Based on the ﬁndings of this study, the following conclusions may be drawn: /C15There was a marked reduction in the compressive strength of the concretes with increasing FA content while the concretes having GGBFS had comparable strength values to that of the control concrete. The SF and MK concretes, on the other hand, had consistently higher compressive strength than the control concrete. /C15The negative effect of FA on the compressive strength was rela- tively diminished with the ternary and quaternary use of min- eral admixtures. /C15According to the GLM-ANOVA result, FA appeared to be most effective factor on the compressive strength; however, the effect of GGBFS appeared to be insigniﬁcant. The contribution of FA, MK, and SF were 26.7%, 12.7%, and 9.7%, respectively. in the compressive strength of the concretes having ternary and/or quaternary blends including FA. However, this adverse effect of FA seemed to be remedied by the combined use of the mineral admix- tures. The concretes with GGBFS and/or SF, on the other hand, had generally comparable strength values to that of the control con- crete. In this group of concrete, no signiﬁcant change was moni- tored in the ternary and quaternary use of mineral admixtures when compared to binary blends of mineral admixtures. Interest- ingly, the concrete incorporating 10% FA and 10% GGBFS achieved the highest compressive strength of this group mixture at 90 days. 3.3. Drying shrinkage The strain developments versus time of drying shrinkage for dif- ferent concretes are presented in Figs. 1–7. Generally, the shrinkage strains were somewhat comparable at very early ages while there was a considerable distinction at later ages of the drying period. It was observed in Fig. 1 that the effects of replacing the PC by FA, GGBFS or MK were to reduce the free shrinkage of SCCs remarkably at w/b ratio of 0.32. When the shrinkage strains measured at 50 days were considered, the shrinkage of the concrete with binary use of PC + MK exhibited the highest reduction in comparison to the control concrete. It was pointed out that higher the replacement le- vel of FA, GGBFS, and MK, higher the reduction in the shrinkage of the concretes. For example, the drying shrinkage of the concretes with 5% and 15% MK were 22% and 26% lower than that of the con- trol concrete, respectively. In the case of FA and GGBFS included concretes, similarly, the former and the latter had lower shrinkage strains by about 14% and 26%, and 5% and 14% at the replacement levels of 20% and 60%, respectively. However, the binary use of SF with PC increased the shrinkage of SCCs with respect to the binary use of FA, GGBFS, MK and control concretes. The inﬂuence of the ternary use of mineral admixtures on the free shrinkage of the SCCs having w/b ratio of 0.32 was demon- strated in Fig. 2. As it is clearly seen in the ﬁgure, all of the mixtures with the ternary blends of the mineral admixtures have low drying shrinkage values when compared to the control concrete. The shrinkage of binary, ternary and quaternary use of FA, GGBFS and SF at w/b ratio of 0.44 was depicted in Figs. 5–7 .A s seen in the ﬁgures, the shrinkage values yielded approximately comparable values at the very early ages of drying period. How- ever, a clear distinction was observed for different concretes after about 10 days. It was clearly observed in Fig. 5 that the concretes with binary blends of FA or GGBFS had lower shrinkage strains than that of the control concrete. This beneﬁcial effect appeared to be more pronounced with increasing the replacement level of mineral admixtures. The concrete having 60% FA had the lowest shrinkage strain. When used at 10% and 15% replacement levels, however, SF enhanced the shrinkage strain of the concretes. The remedy to diminish the adverse effect of SF on the shrinkage may be the use of this mineral within ternary and/or quaternary blends. Indeed, Figs. 6 and 7 clearly exhibited that the control concrete had the highest shrinkage strain. The combined use of the mineral admixtures fairly reduced the negative inﬂuence of SF. 3.4. Statistical evaluation of test results In order to examine the variations in the compressive strength and drying shrinkage of the SCCs in a quantitative manner, a gen- eral linear model analysis of variance (GLM-ANOVA) was per- formed at a 0.05 level of signiﬁcance. GLM-ANOVA is an important statistical analysis and diagnostic tool which helps in reducing the error variance and quantiﬁes the dominance of a con- trol factor. To perform the GLM-ANOVA, compressive strength and drying shrinkage of the SCCs were assigned as the dependent vari- ables while the types of the mineral admixtures were the factors. Then, the statistical analysis was performed and the percentage contribution of the each mineral admixture on compressive strength and drying shrinkage was determined. The contributions of the mineral admixtures on the measured drying shrinkage are presented in Table 5 .
